stem: What does a red triangular sign with a point at the top usually indicate in the Netherlands?

choices.text: A warning of a hazard ahead

explanation.short: Triangular signs with a red border are warning signs and alert you to a hazard ahead.
explanation.examTip: Shape and colour tell you the category before you even read the symbol.

image.alt: A red-bordered equilateral triangle pointing upward — the standard shape for Dutch warning signs.
image.caption: Warning sign — red upward-pointing triangle

stem: A round sign with a red border and a white background generally means:

explanation.short: Round signs with a red border are prohibitory: they tell you what you may not do.
explanation.examTip: Red borders almost always restrict you. Blue rounds, by contrast, give a mandatory instruction.

image.alt: A round sign with a thick red border on a white background — the standard form of a Dutch prohibitory sign.
image.caption: Prohibitory sign — round with red border

stem: What does a round blue sign typically indicate?

explanation.short: Round blue signs are mandatory: they oblige you to follow the indicated action or direction.
explanation.examTip: Compare with rectangular blue signs, which are informational rather than mandatory.

image.alt: A round blue sign with a white arrow, representing the mandatory category of Dutch road signs.
image.caption: Mandatory sign — round blue

stem: You see a yellow diamond-shaped sign with a white border. What does it mean?

explanation.short: The yellow diamond is the priority-road sign: traffic on this road has priority at intersections until cancelled.
explanation.examTip: The same sign with a black diagonal stripe means the priority road ends.

image.alt: A yellow diamond-shaped sign with a white border indicating priority road status.
image.caption: Priority road sign — yellow diamond

stem: An inverted (point-down) triangle with a red border at an intersection means:

choices.text: Give way to traffic on the crossing road

explanation.short: The inverted triangle is the give-way (B6) sign: yield to traffic on the crossing road, but you do not have to stop if it is clear.
explanation.examTip: If the sign were octagonal and red (STOP), you would have to come to a full stop even with no traffic.

image.alt: An inverted triangle with a red border and white background — the Dutch give-way sign.
image.caption: Give-way sign — inverted red-bordered triangle

stem: A round red sign with a single horizontal white bar means:

choices.text: No entry for vehicular traffic in this direction

explanation.short: This is the C2 sign: no entry for drivers in that direction, often marking a one-way street from the wrong end.
explanation.examTip: Pedestrians and sometimes cyclists may still be allowed; check additional plates underneath.

stem: A round white sign with a red border showing the number 50 means:

explanation.short: A red-bordered round speed sign sets the maximum legal speed on that road.
explanation.examTip: Minimum speed is shown on a blue round sign; recommended speed on a rectangular sign.

image.alt: A round white sign with a red border showing the number 50, the maximum-speed-limit sign.
image.caption: Maximum speed limit — 50 km/h

stem: What does a continuous white line in the centre of the road mean?

choices.text: You may overtake if it is safe
You may not cross or straddle the line

explanation.short: A solid centre line may not be crossed or straddled, except in narrowly defined situations such as avoiding an obstacle.
explanation.examTip: A broken line allows overtaking; a combination line follows the rule on your own side.

image.alt: Plan view of a two-way road with a continuous solid white line down the centre; a red no-crossing symbol shows the line may not be crossed or straddled.
image.caption: Solid centre line — do not cross or straddle

stem: Triangular markings on the road pointing toward you ("shark's teeth") mean:

choices.text: You must give way to traffic on the crossing road

explanation.short: Shark's teeth indicate that you must yield to crossing traffic, even if no give-way sign is visible.
explanation.examTip: They often appear at exits of roundabouts and where cycle paths cross a road.

image.alt: A row of white triangles painted across the lane pointing toward the driver, with the crossing road ahead — the give-way 'shark's teeth' marking.
image.caption: Shark's teeth — give way to the crossing road

stem: A rectangular blue sign in the Netherlands generally provides:

explanation.short: Rectangular blue signs are informational: parking, one-way streets, route guidance, and similar.
explanation.examTip: Round blue signs are mandatory; rectangular blue signs only inform.

stem: You see a warning sign for children. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.text: Maintain your speed and stay alert
Reduce speed and be ready to stop

explanation.short: Warning signs require you to adjust speed and attention so you can react if the hazard appears.
explanation.examTip: Warnings are not hard rules but the examiner expects a visible adjustment in speed or scanning.

stem: A round sign with a red border showing a red car alongside a black car means:

explanation.short: Two cars side-by-side inside a red-bordered circle is the overtaking prohibition for cars.
explanation.examTip: The same sign with the cars in grey on a grey background marks the end of the ban.

stem: A round blue sign with a single red diagonal stroke means:

choices.text: No parking, but you may stop briefly to load or drop off

explanation.short: One diagonal stroke prohibits parking but allows a short stop for loading or letting passengers in or out.
explanation.examTip: Two crossed strokes mean no stopping at all — neither parking nor a brief halt is permitted.

stem: A round blue sign showing a white bicycle means:

choices.text: Cyclists are warned of a hazard
Compulsory cycle path — cyclists must use it

explanation.short: A round blue sign with a bicycle marks a compulsory cycle path, which cyclists are required to use.
explanation.examTip: A rectangular blue sign with a bicycle marks an optional cycle lane, not a compulsory path.

image.alt: A red octagonal sign with the white word STOP across the middle.
image.caption: The STOP sign — full stop required, every time.

stem: At a red octagonal STOP sign you must:

choices.text: Slow down and yield only if other traffic is close
Come to a complete stop, then proceed when safe
Stop only if a police officer is present
Treat it as a give-way sign

explanation.short: A STOP sign always requires a full stop at the line, even when the crossing road is empty.
explanation.examTip: Failing to stop fully is one of the most common reasons to fail the practical exam.

stem: A rectangular sign shows a red arrow pointing up and a smaller black arrow pointing down. What does it mean?

choices.text: You have priority over oncoming traffic
You must give way to oncoming traffic

explanation.short: When your arrow is red on a priority-narrowing sign, you must wait and let oncoming traffic pass first.
explanation.examTip: Read the colour of your own arrow: red yields, black has priority.

stem: A rectangular sign with the word 'zone' under a 30 km/h symbol means:

choices.text: The 30 km/h limit only applies on this street
The 30 km/h limit applies in the whole area until an end-of-zone sign
30 km/h is only a recommended speed

explanation.short: A zone sign extends the limit to all roads inside the zone until you pass the matching end-of-zone sign.
explanation.examTip: An ordinary speed sign without 'zone' only applies until the next intersection or speed sign.

stem: A square sign with a blue background and a white number such as 70 means:

explanation.short: A square or rectangular speed sign gives a recommended, not legally enforced, speed — often before a bend.
explanation.examTip: Maximum speed is round with a red border; minimum speed is round with a solid blue background.

stem: A continuous white line at the outer edge of the carriageway means:

choices.text: An extra lane you may drive in when busy
An emergency lane reserved for emergency vehicles only
The edge of the driving surface — the area outside is not a lane

explanation.short: A continuous edge line marks where the carriageway ends; the strip beyond it is not a driving lane.
explanation.examTip: On motorways the hard shoulder is for emergencies only; on other roads the strip outside the edge line is generally for cyclists or pedestrians.

image.alt: Plan view of a carriageway with a continuous solid white line at its outer edge and a hatched strip beyond it that is not a driving lane.
image.caption: Continuous edge line — marks the edge of the carriageway

stem: Your lane has a white arrow painted on the road pointing only to the right. What must you do?

choices.text: You may go straight or turn right
You must turn right at the next junction
The arrow is only a suggestion

explanation.short: A directional arrow in a lane is mandatory: you must follow the indicated direction at the next junction.
explanation.examTip: If the arrow does not match your route, change lanes well before the junction while it is still allowed.

image.alt: A driving lane with a white arrow painted on the road surface pointing only to the right, requiring drivers to turn right at the next junction.
image.caption: Mandatory lane arrow — you must turn right

stem: You see a triangular sign with a red border showing a slippery-road symbol and a sub-plate 'next 3 km'. The road looks dry. What should you do?

choices.text: Ignore the sign because the road is clearly dry
Brake hard immediately as a precaution
Ease off the accelerator, increase following distance and be ready to brake
Stop and check the surface before proceeding

explanation.short: A warning sign requires you to reduce speed and attention, even if the hazard is not visible at this moment.
explanation.examTip: A warning sign is placed because the surface becomes unpredictable, not because it is wet at this exact spot.

stem: You pass a 'no overtaking' sign on a rural road. Two kilometres later you have passed one intersection but seen no end-of-restriction sign. May you now overtake?

choices.text: No — only the explicit end sign cancels the prohibition
Yes — the intersection cancels the prohibition unless a new sign restates it
Only if you can see at least 500 metres ahead
Only if no vehicle is behind you

explanation.short: A prohibition is normally cancelled by the next intersection unless restated by a new sign.
explanation.examTip: 'I have driven a long way' is not enough — an intersection or a matching end sign is needed.

stem: You approach a junction with shark's-teeth markings painted on the road but no priority sign on a pole. What is the rule?

choices.text: You have priority because there is no sign
You must yield — shark's-teeth markings give way on their own
You must come to a full stop, as for a STOP sign
Right-has-priority applies because no sign is present

explanation.short: Shark's-teeth road markings act as a give-way sign on their own; no pole sign is required.
explanation.examTip: Markings can carry the same legal force as signs — read them with equal attention.

stem: You enter a '30 zone' near a school. Two hundred metres later you turn into a quiet side street with no speed-limit sign on it. What is the limit there?

choices.text: 50 km/h — the side street is not signed
30 km/h — the zone covers all roads inside it until the end-of-zone sign
Whatever the built-up area limit is, since no sign restates 30
No legal limit applies in side streets

explanation.short: A zone sign applies to every road inside the zone until you pass the matching end-of-zone sign.
explanation.examTip: The absence of a repeat sign inside a zone does not lift the limit.
